q: You are developing a Lambda function that needs to call a DynamoDB table. The function requires temporary AWS credentials. Which service should you use?

36 hours
1 hour

buildspec.yml

Canary

BatchGetItem and BatchWriteItem operations

Use Lambda provisioned concurrency or ECS fargate for guaranteed performance

q: A developer deploys an application via CodeDeploy to a fleet of EC2 instances. The deployment fails with a 'No deployable instances found' error. The EC2 instances were recently launched from a new AMI. What is the MOST LIKELY cause?
options: The CodeDeploy agent is not installed or not running on the new EC2 instances — the agent must be pre-installed in the AMI or bootstrapped via EC2 user data
The deployment group was configured with a BlueGreen deployment type, which requires at least two Availability Zones
The EC2 instances are using an instance type not supported by CodeDeploy (t4g.nano)
CodeDeploy requires instances to be in the same VPC as the CodeDeploy service endpoint
explain: CodeDeploy requires the CodeDeploy agent to be installed and actively running on each target EC2 instance before a deployment can proceed. When launching from a new AMI, the agent must be included in the AMI or installed via EC2 user data/Systems Manager. If the agent is absent or stopped, CodeDeploy cannot communicate with the instance and reports 'No deployable instances found'. The other options are not valid constraints — CodeDeploy works across AZs, all common instance types, and uses HTTPS outbound (not a VPC peering requirement).

q: A Lambda function is configured with an SQS event source mapping but is not processing messages that are visible in the queue. The Lambda function itself works correctly when invoked manually. What is the MOST LIKELY cause?
options: The Lambda function timeout is set to 3 seconds, which is less than the SQS message retention period
The Lambda reserved concurrency is set to 0, preventing any invocations from the event source mapping
The SQS queue uses server-side encryption with a customer-managed KMS key, and the Lambda execution role lacks kms:Decrypt permission on that key
SQS Standard queues do not support Lambda event source mappings — only SQS FIFO queues are supported
explain: When Lambda reserved concurrency is set to 0, the function cannot be invoked by any trigger including SQS event source mappings. The event source mapping remains enabled and polls the queue, but all invocation attempts are throttled immediately. Messages accumulate in the queue and eventually reach the dead-letter queue (if configured) or expire. Setting reserved concurrency to at least 1, or removing the concurrency limit, resolves the issue. KMS encryption can also block polling but requires specific IAM errors, not silent non-processing.

q: You need to generate a signed URL for an S3 object that expires in 1 hour. How should you do this?

AWS::ApiGateway::RestApi
AWS::Serverless::Function
AWS::Serverless::Endpoint

q: A Lambda function deployed inside a VPC makes outbound HTTPS calls to a third-party payment API. The function works correctly in development (no VPC) but consistently times out in production (VPC-attached). The function has a 30-second timeout and the payment API responds within 2 seconds. What is the MOST LIKELY cause?
options: Lambda functions in a VPC can only communicate with AWS services via VPC endpoints and cannot reach external internet APIs at all
Lambda functions inside a VPC require a NAT Gateway or NAT Instance in a public subnet to reach internet endpoints — without it, outbound internet traffic is blocked
The Lambda function needs an Elastic IP address attached to its network interface to make outbound internet calls from a VPC
VPC-attached Lambda functions have a hard limit of 5 seconds for outbound HTTPS connections regardless of the function timeout setting
explain: Lambda functions attached to a VPC route outbound traffic through VPC networking rules. By default, a VPC has no internet access. To reach internet endpoints, the Lambda function's subnet must route traffic through a NAT Gateway (or NAT Instance) located in a public subnet, which has an Internet Gateway attached. Without this NAT path, all outbound internet connections are silently dropped — causing timeouts rather than explicit errors. The other options are incorrect: there is no 5-second VPC timeout limit, Elastic IPs are for inbound access not outbound NAT, and Lambda can reach the internet with proper VPC routing.

5 minutes
15 minutes

LRU policy is not effective for session data

Lambda authorizer (custom)

q: A Kinesis consumer needs to read all records from the beginning of a stream. What GetShardIterator type should be used?
options: LATEST to get the newest records
AT_TIMESTAMP to read from a specific time
NEW_IMAGE to get only changed records
TRIM_HORIZON to read from the oldest retained record

q: Your Elastic Beanstalk application needs to run worker tasks in the background. What should you use?

CodeDeploy
Lambda functions
Additional EC2 instances
explain: Elastic Beanstalk Worker environment tier automatically pulls messages from SQS and runs them.

q: DynamoDB table shows low throughput despite high provisioned capacity. What could cause this?
options: Too many indexes on the table
Hot partition due to uneven key distribution
CloudWatch Logs consuming capacity
Encryption overhead
explain: Hot partitions occur when many requests target the same partition key, causing throttling despite high capacity.

q: You are building a serverless application with Lambda and need persistent storage for application state. What should you use?
options: DynamoDB or RDS
Lambda /tmp directory
S3 with versioning
CloudWatch Logs
explain: DynamoDB or RDS provide persistent, reliable storage for application state across Lambda invocations.

q: Your microservices are instrumented with X-Ray. A distributed trace shows long latency in the downstream service. How should you trace internal operations?
options: Create X-Ray subsegments to measure specific operations within the service
Use CloudTrail to audit API calls
Add more logging to CloudWatch
Use X-Ray annotations to tag high-level information
explain: Subsegments break down service execution into named operations (database queries, API calls), revealing where latency occurs.

q: You need to deploy Lambda functions with infrastructure as code. Should you use CloudFormation or SAM?
options: Both are equivalent
CloudFormation only
Neither, use the Console
SAM for simpler Lambda-focused deployments
explain: SAM provides Lambda-specific optimizations and simpler syntax, while CloudFormation is more general.

q: You need to filter X-Ray traces to see only those with errors. Where should error information be added to the trace?
options: Use annotations with error flags for filtering
Use CloudWatch Logs Insights to search error traces
Add error=true as metadata in the trace segment
In the response object returned to the client
explain: Annotations are indexed and filterable in X-Ray console, making them ideal for error flags, status codes, and custom tags.

q: A developer builds a real-time notification system where an EventBridge rule triggers a Lambda function on specific events. The Lambda function occasionally fails due to transient downstream service errors. The developer needs failed events to be retried and unprocessable events to not be lost. Which configuration handles this?
options: Configure EventBridge retry policy on the rule target with maxEventAge and retryAttempts — EventBridge automatically retries failed Lambda invocations up to 185 times over 24 hours before sending to a Dead Letter Queue
Configure a Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) on the EventBridge rule target — failed events after all retry attempts are delivered to an SQS DLQ for inspection and manual reprocessing
Add manual retry logic inside the Lambda function using a try-catch loop — this avoids needing EventBridge retry configuration

Configure both a retry policy (retryAttempts=3, maxEventAge=3600) AND a Dead Letter Queue on the EventBridge target — retries handle transient failures and the DLQ captures events that exhaust all retries
explain: The complete solution combines both retry policy and DLQ on the EventBridge target. The retry policy handles transient failures by retrying the Lambda invocation (up to 185 retries over 24 hours). If a event exhausts all retries or exceeds maxEventAge, EventBridge sends it to the configured DLQ (SQS queue) — ensuring no events are permanently lost. Using only a retry policy loses events after exhaustion. Using only a DLQ skips the automatic retry benefit. Manual retry in Lambda doesn't handle cases where Lambda itself can't be invoked.

q: You want S3 to automatically notify a Lambda function whenever a new image file is uploaded. How should this be configured?
options: Enable S3 versioning to track new uploads
Manually invoke Lambda after each upload
Use CloudWatch Events to poll S3
Use S3 event notifications with Lambda destination
explain: S3 event notifications (ObjectCreated) can trigger Lambda directly when matching objects are uploaded.

q: Your S3 bucket has objects with different retention requirements: 30 days for logs, 1 year for archives. How are these enforced?
options: Manually delete old objects
Use bucket policies to prevent deletion
Apply S3 lifecycle rules with different prefixes or tags for each retention period
Use S3 Glacier for all objects
explain: Lifecycle rules can transition/delete objects based on age and tags, automating retention policy enforcement.

q: You need to ensure a Lambda function has at least 100 concurrent invocations available at all times for critical workload. What configuration prevents scaling delays?
options: Increase timeout to ensure functions complete
Enable concurrent execution without limits
Use reserved concurrency set to 100 to keep capacity warm
Use Spot instances for Lambda
explain: Reserved concurrency guarantees minimum available concurrency, preventing throttling and cold starts for critical applications.

q: Your Lambda function calls external APIs with synchronous invocation. If the API times out, the function fails. How should this be handled with async patterns?
options: Increase Lambda timeout to match API timeout
Use Step Functions instead
Call the API multiple times in parallel
Use SQS intermediate queue for async invocation with error retry mechanism
explain: SQS provides automatic retries, dead-letter handling, and decouples caller from external API failures.

q: You need to implement distributed tracing across microservices. Which service should you use?
options: EventBridge
AWS X-Ray
CloudWatch Metrics
CloudWatch Logs
explain: X-Ray provides distributed tracing and visualization of request flows through microservices.

q: A Lambda function's memory is set to 128MB but processing a 500MB file causes memory errors. Moving to 3008MB fixes it but increases cost. What is the optimal approach?
options: Keep 3008MB since it now works
Split the file into smaller parts externally
Use Glue instead of Lambda
Stream the file using chunked reading with 512MB memory, reducing cost while handling large files
explain: Streaming/chunking allows processing large files with modest memory; reading entire files into memory is inefficient.

q: A developer maintains an Elastic Beanstalk application that must use different database endpoints, log levels, and feature flags per environment (dev, staging, production). The values cannot be committed to source control. Which Elastic Beanstalk mechanism is the AWS-recommended approach for managing environment-specific configuration without code changes?
options: Use Elastic Beanstalk environment properties (environment variables) configured per environment in the EB console or .ebextensions, keeping sensitive values out of source control
Maintain separate branches in CodeCommit for each environment and hardcode the configuration in each branch's application.properties file
Store all configuration values in a shared AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store path and have the application load them at startup based on an APP_ENV variable
Package separate application bundles per environment using CodeBuild, embedding environment-specific values at build time
explain: Elastic Beanstalk environment properties are the native mechanism for environment-specific configuration. They are set per EB environment (dev vs. staging vs. production) via the console, CLI, or .ebextensions, and are injected as OS environment variables at runtime — keeping sensitive values out of source code. Systems Manager Parameter Store is also valid but adds a dependency; EB environment properties are simpler and built-in. Hardcoding values in branches creates branching complexity. Embedding values at build time prevents the same artifact from being promoted across environments.

q: You deploy a Lambda function that reads secrets from Secrets Manager on every invocation. Latency is high and API calls are expensive. What optimization helps?
options: Use IAM roles instead of secrets
Cache secrets in the function initialization code outside the handler
Move to environment variables
Pre-load all secrets into DynamoDB
explain: Initialization code runs once per container; caching secrets there avoids repeated API calls across warm invocations.

q: You need to cache API responses to reduce latency. Which service should you use?
options: S3
DynamoDB
ElastiCache
RDS
explain: ElastiCache provides in-memory caching for frequently accessed data with low latency.

q: A Lambda function uses provisioned concurrency set to 10. However, peak traffic requires 50 concurrent invocations. What should happen to additional invocations beyond 10?
options: 10 use provisioned (warm), 40 more scale up with auto-scaling (may experience cold starts)
The function is queued indefinitely
Only 10 requests are processed; rest are dropped
All requests are throttled
explain: Provisioned concurrency starts 10 warm instances; excess traffic auto-scales with potential cold starts beyond provisioned level.

q: Your DynamoDB table has millions of items. You need to query by both userId and status. You already have a GSI on userId. Should you add another GSI on status?
options: Create LSI on status instead
No, create a GSI on (userId, status) composite key
Use scan with filter
Yes, add GSI on status
explain: A composite GSI with userId as partition key and status as sort key enables efficient queries on both attributes without additional indexes.

q: An X-Ray service map shows a Lambda function with a consistently high error rate. The errors started appearing after a recent code deployment. A developer needs to identify the root cause quickly. Which X-Ray feature provides the MOST TARGETED insight into what changed?
options: X-Ray Sampling rules — increase the sampling rate to 100% to capture every request and reduce the time to identify the pattern
X-Ray Groups with filter expressions — create a group filtering for error=true to isolate only error traces and examine their segments
X-Ray Insights — automatically detects anomalies in error rates compared to a baseline and surfaces the root cause segment
X-Ray Analytics — use the response time distribution histogram to compare latency before and after the deployment
explain: X-Ray Insights automatically monitors your application's trace data, detects statistically significant anomalies (such as a sudden spike in error rates after a deployment), and pinpoints the specific segment or downstream dependency causing the problem. It compares current behavior against a historical baseline and surfaces actionable findings without requiring manual filter construction. While X-Ray Groups and Analytics are useful for manual investigation, Insights provides the fastest automated root-cause identification — especially valuable when correlating a change in error rate to a deployment event.

q: You configure a Lambda function with destinations for success (SNS) and failure (SQS DLQ). An async invocation succeeds; which destination receives the event?
options: Only SQS because it's a DLQ
Both SNS and SQS
Neither; destinations are for errors only
Only SNS because it's configured for success
explain: Destinations route based on invocation outcome: success events go to success destination, failures to the DLQ destination.

q: Your Lambda function is URL-based for easy invocation. The invoker is in a different AWS account. What permission must be granted?
options: Lambda:InvokeFunctionUrl permission to the other account
Full S3 access to both accounts
Lambda:InvokeFunction only
No permission needed for same region
explain: Cross-account Lambda URL invocation requires explicit lambda:InvokeFunctionUrl permission in the function's resource-based policy.

q: You are deploying a web application to Fargate. Which networking configuration is required?
options: Fargate doesn't require network configuration
VPC, subnet, and security group for the task
EC2 security groups
Network ACLs only
explain: Fargate tasks require VPC configuration with subnets and security groups for network connectivity.

q: API Gateway method call shows a sudden increase in latency. What could cause this?
options: Database query optimization issues
Backend Lambda scaling up
Any of the above
Network congestion
explain: Latency increases can come from Lambda cold starts, network issues, or database bottlenecks.

q: You need to process CSV file uploads asynchronously. What architecture should you use?
options: API Gateway endpoint with SQS
S3 upload triggering Lambda via event notification
CodeCommit repository
Upload directly to Lambda
explain: S3 event notifications trigger Lambda to process uploaded files immediately without synchronous waiting.

q: A Lambda function processes events from DynamoDB Streams but some events are missed. The batch window and batch size settings are default. What could cause missed events?
options: Batch window is too large
Lambda concurrency is set too low for stream shards; increase concurrent execution or shard count
Events are automatically deleted after 1 hour
DynamoDB Streams has insufficient capacity
explain: DynamoDB Streams source mapping has one invocation per shard; low concurrency causes single-shard backlog and event loss.

q: Your CodePipeline needs to approve deployments based on manual review. Where should you add this?
options: In the Build stage
In the Source stage
As a separate stage with Manual approval action
In the Deploy stage only
explain: Manual approval is a separate stage action that stops the pipeline for human review.

q: You need to invoke a Lambda function from a Kinesis stream but want to filter records before invocation. Where should filtering happen?
options: Use CloudWatch Events to filter
Pre-filter in Kinesis
Inside the Lambda function
Configure filter policies in the event source mapping to reduce invocations
explain: Event source mapping filter policies allow server-side filtering, reducing unnecessary Lambda invocations and costs.

q: You need to generate unique IDs for database records. What is the best approach?
options: Use DynamoDB auto-incrementing IDs
Use RDS with SERIAL type
Generate UUIDs in application code
Use DynamoDB with UUID generation
explain: DynamoDB doesn't auto-increment; use UUID generation in application code or use separate counter table.

q: Your Lambda function needs to access another AWS service. What should you configure?
options: Lambda layer with service SDK
IAM role with permissions for that service
VPC endpoint to the service
Environment variables with service endpoints
explain: IAM role attached to Lambda must have permissions for the services it needs to call.

q: Your application metrics show DynamoDB read throttling. What is the best solution?
options: Use DAX caching for frequently accessed items
Increase provisioned read capacity
Enable auto-scaling on the table
Any of the above depending on the pattern
explain: Solutions depend on access patterns: scale capacity, auto-scale, or use caching strategically.

q: You need to process a stream of IoT sensor data with Lambda. Events come frequently. Some processing takes longer than expected, causing queue buildup. What should you configure?
options: Increase Lambda timeout
Increase Lambda memory
Increase the batch size for event source mapping
Decrease batch window to process faster
explain: Larger batches allow Lambda to process more events per invocation, reducing the number of invocations needed and improving throughput for streaming workloads.

q: You're designing a DynamoDB table for user sessions where SessionId is the partition key. Users are distributed globally. Why might a few users experience throttling while others don't?
options: Network latency for distant regions
Backup operations are running
Some session IDs hash to the same partition, creating hot partitions with uneven load
Users with longer sessions use more throughput
explain: Partition key distribution matters; poor distribution leads to hot partitions and throttling despite adequate total capacity.

q: A DynamoDB table uses on-demand billing. Traffic spikes cause occasional throttling. What is the cause and solution?
options: Items are too large
The partition key is poorly chosen
On-demand should never throttle
On-demand billing mode has adaptive throughput limits; exceeding limits causes throttling - use provisioned with auto-scaling
explain: Even on-demand billing has throughput limits (40,000 RCU/WCU per partition key); sustained spikes require provisioned capacity.

q: You need to atomically increment a counter in DynamoDB without reading the current value. What operation should be used?
options: GetItem then UpdateItem separately
Use Lambda for atomic increment
Query to get current value then write
UpdateExpression with SET counter = counter + :val
explain: UpdateExpression with SET allows atomic server-side increment without read-modify-write race conditions.

q: Your DynamoDB query retrieves 100 items but you only need 10. Each item is 10KB. The query returns all 100. How is this optimized?
options: Remove unused attributes from the table
Add Limit parameter to the query to retrieve only 10 items upfront
Use ProjectionExpression to exclude large attributes
Query returns items as needed; no optimization possible
explain: Limit parameter stops retrieval after N items, reducing data transfer and improving query latency and cost.

q: Lambda function throws 'Unable to import module' error. What is the likely cause?
options: Missing dependency in Lambda layer or deployment package
Lambda timeout
Incorrect handler configuration
Missing IAM permissions
explain: This error means a required module/library is not available in the Lambda package or layer.

q: You need to ensure your Lambda function doesn't exceed memory limits. What should you do?
options: Use Lambda layers for all code
Always allocate maximum memory
Monitor CloudWatch metrics and optimize code
Split code across multiple functions
explain: Monitor metrics, identify memory leaks, and optimize code to stay within limits and reduce costs.

q: A DynamoDB table has a Global Secondary Index (GSI) on a sparse attribute (present in 5% of items). Queries on the GSI are slow. What is the issue?
options: Table size is too large
GSI on sparse attributes can cause hot partitions if sparse attribute values are unevenly distributed
GSI only works on frequently used attributes
GSI are always slower than queries on main table
explain: Sparse attributes in GSI can lead to skewed partition distribution if values cluster; normal queries are better for sparse data.

q: An Elastic Beanstalk environment shows 'Degraded' health status. The application was working correctly before a recent configuration change. The developer needs to identify the specific issue. Which Elastic Beanstalk feature provides the MOST DETAILED diagnostic information?
options: AWS Trusted Advisor checks — identifies common Elastic Beanstalk misconfigurations and security issues
Elastic Beanstalk Enhanced Health Reporting — provides instance-level health factors including HTTP response codes, CPU, and application latency with cause descriptions
Elastic Beanstalk environment events — events log shows the last 10 configuration changes with timestamps
EC2 instance metrics in CloudWatch — CPU and network graphs show resource utilization at the instance level
explain: Elastic Beanstalk Enhanced Health Reporting (requires the enhanced health agent) provides granular, instance-level health data including HTTP 4xx/5xx rates, application latency, CPU utilization, and specific cause descriptions (e.g., '15% of requests are failing with HTTP 500'). This is far more actionable than generic CloudWatch metrics or event logs. When a configuration change degrades health, Enhanced Health Reporting identifies which instances are unhealthy and why — pointing directly to whether the issue is application-level, infrastructure-level, or configuration-related.

q: Your API needs to generate documents (PDFs) on demand. Documents can take 30+ seconds to generate. Clients expect fast responses. What architecture should you use?
options: Use EC2 for document processing
Invoke Lambda asynchronously, store in S3, return presigned URL immediately
Generate synchronously in Lambda
Use Step Functions to coordinate generation
explain: Asynchronous processing with S3 storage and presigned URLs provides fast API responses while generating documents in the background.

q: You are refactoring a monolith into microservices using Lambda. Service A needs to call Service B synchronously. What should you use to handle transient failures?
options: SQS with Lambda long polling
Direct HTTP calls with exponential backoff
Step Functions for orchestration
SQS for reliable async messaging
explain: For synchronous service-to-service calls with transient failures, HTTP with SDK retry strategy and exponential backoff is appropriate.

q: You need to fetch items from multiple logical groups in a single request. DynamoDB supports this via what operation?
options: Query with multiple partition keys
Global Secondary Index
Scan with filters
BatchGetItem to retrieve items from different partitions in one request
explain: BatchGetItem allows fetching up to 100 items across different partition keys in a single request, more efficient than individual GetItems.

q: You need to deploy Lambda functions with dependencies. What is the best practice?
options: Include all dependencies in the deployment package
Install dependencies at runtime in the function
Upload dependencies to S3
Use Lambda layers for dependencies
explain: Lambda layers provide a clean way to manage shared dependencies across multiple functions.

q: A DynamoDB stream is configured with NEW_AND_OLD_IMAGES view type. A Lambda consumer processes stream records but only needs to know what changed, not the full record. What attribute provides this?
options: Streams don't support change detection
Use CloudTrail instead
Use DynamoDBStreamRecord.dynamodb.OldImage and NewImage to compute diff
DynamoDBStreamRecord.dynamodb.Keys only
explain: Comparing OldImage and NewImage reveals attribute changes; DynamoDB Streams don't provide explicit deltas.

q: Your application uses both strong consistency (immediately after write) and eventual consistency reads. How should consistency be configured per query?
options: Strong consistency is not available
Set ConsistentRead=true for strong consistency in GetItem or Query
DynamoDB handles consistency automatically
Use a separate table for consistent reads
explain: ConsistentRead parameter in GetItem/Query/BatchGetItem enables strong vs eventual consistency per operation.

q: You're implementing a leaderboard that must show top 100 scores globally. Scores are stored in DynamoDB with UserId as partition key. How should queries work efficiently?
options: Scan the entire table and sort in application
Create GSI with Score as partition key, sorted by Score descending, then Query and limit to 100
Use Redshift for leaderboards
Query UserId then scan all items
explain: GSI with partition key on Score allows efficient ranking queries without scanning the entire table.

q: Your Lambda function uses a database connection pool that is being reinitialized on every invocation causing performance degradation. How should you fix this?
options: Move connection pool initialization outside the handler (into global scope)
Use RDS Proxy instead
Increase Lambda memory
Use DynamoDB instead of database
explain: Lambda reuses container instances across invocations. Initializing resources outside the handler ensures they persist across calls within the same container.

q: A Step Functions state machine executes a Lambda function in a Task state. The Lambda function starts but the state machine execution never advances past that state and eventually times out after 1 year (the default). The Lambda function logs show it completes successfully. What is the MOST LIKELY cause?
options: The Task state is using the .waitForTaskToken integration pattern, and the Lambda function is not calling SendTaskSuccess with the task token before exiting
The Lambda function returns a response larger than 256 KB, which exceeds the Step Functions payload size limit and causes silent failure
The IAM role attached to the state machine lacks lambda:InvokeFunction permission, causing the invocation to silently time out
Step Functions has a hard limit of 25 concurrent state machine executions — additional executions queue and appear to stall
explain: The .waitForTaskToken integration pattern pauses the state machine and waits indefinitely for an external signal via SendTaskSuccess or SendTaskFailure with the task token. If the Lambda function receives the task token but exits without calling back to Step Functions, the state machine waits forever (up to the state's HeartbeatSeconds or TimeoutSeconds if configured, otherwise the execution timeout). This is a common mistake when migrating from the default RequestResponse pattern to the callback pattern. The fix is to ensure the Lambda function always calls step-functions:SendTaskSuccess or SendTaskFailure before returning.

q: You need to send a message to multiple SQS queues from Lambda. Currently you are making sequential calls. How should you optimize this?
options: Create multiple Lambda functions
Use SQS batch SendMessage API
Use DynamoDB Streams instead
Use SNS to fan out to multiple SQS queues
explain: SNS fanout pattern sends a single message to multiple SQS queues, and SNS batch API reduces API calls compared to sequential queue writes.

q: You need to store configuration that changes frequently. Where should you store it?
options: In hardcoded environment variables
In CloudFormation parameters
In the Lambda function code
In SSM Parameter Store or AppConfig
explain: SSM Parameter Store and AppConfig allow dynamic configuration changes without code redeployment.

q: You're implementing API rate limiting to protect against abuse. Which API Gateway feature controls requests per time period?
options: VPC Endpoint policies
Usage Plans with quota and rate settings
WAF rules only
Throttling with RateLimit and BurstLimit settings
explain: RateLimit (requests per second) and BurstLimit control throttling at the API level and per client with API Keys.

q: Your application uses API Gateway REST API that needs to validate complex JSON schemas for incoming requests. Current validation is happening in Lambda. Where should this move?
options: Add CloudFront validation
Use API Gateway HTTP API instead
Keep in Lambda
Add request validators and models to API Gateway
explain: API Gateway request validators can reject invalid requests before reaching Lambda, reducing unnecessary invocations and improving response times.

q: You need to implement pagination for a DynamoDB query that can return thousands of results. The API should return batches of 50 items. What should you implement?
options: Use Scan operation with paging
Use Query API with Limit parameter and ExclusiveStartKey for pagination
Query all items and slice in Lambda
Implement offset-based pagination
explain: DynamoDB Query with Limit and ExclusiveStartKey enables efficient pagination without loading all items into memory.

q: Your API receives CSV requests but Lambda expects JSON. How should API Gateway handle conversion?
options: Use request mapping template to parse CSV into JSON structure
Lambda must accept CSV
API Gateway cannot transform formats
Create separate CSV and JSON endpoints
explain: VTL mapping templates can parse CSV, XML, or other formats and transform into Lambda-compatible structures.

q: An API method has a Lambda integration that returns status code 200 but API Gateway returns 500 to the client. What is the issue?
options: Lambda response format doesn't match expected statusCode/body structure in integration response
API Gateway instance is overloaded
CloudFront cache is stale
Lambda function is broken
explain: Lambda proxy integration expects {statusCode, body, headers} format; missing or malformed response causes 502 Bad Gateway or 500 errors.

q: You need to cache API responses for 5 minutes to reduce backend load. Where should caching be configured?
options: In API Gateway method response cache settings with TTL=300 seconds
In Lambda function
Caching requires external database
Use CloudFront in front of API
explain: API Gateway method caching with TTL caches responses per cache key (query params, headers), reducing backend invocations.

q: A serverless application uses AWS Lambda functions that query an Amazon RDS MySQL database. Under high traffic, Lambda's concurrency spikes to 500 simultaneous executions, exhausting the RDS max_connections limit (151) and causing 'Too many connections' errors. The engineering team needs a durable solution that scales without modifying the application or increasing RDS instance size. Which service resolves this?
options: Place Amazon RDS Proxy between Lambda and RDS; it pools and multiplexes database connections so hundreds of Lambda functions share a small pool of actual RDS connections
Enable RDS Multi-AZ to distribute read connections across the standby replica, doubling the effective connection limit
Increase the Lambda reserved concurrency limit to 50 so fewer simultaneous executions connect to the database at the same time
Switch from RDS MySQL to Amazon DynamoDB, which does not have a connection limit and scales automatically
explain: Amazon RDS Proxy is purpose-built for serverless workloads. It maintains a warm pool of long-lived database connections and multiplexes thousands of Lambda invocations onto that pool. This eliminates 'Too many connections' errors without changing application code or resizing RDS. Multi-AZ standby does not serve read traffic — it is a failover replica only. Reducing Lambda concurrency limits throughput and hurts user experience. Switching to DynamoDB requires significant application rewrite and is not appropriate for existing relational workloads.

q: Your Lambda function creates temporary files during processing. You need to ensure cleanup happens even if processing fails. What is the best approach?
options: Delete files at the end of handler
Set /tmp to auto-cleanup
Use Lambda layers for cleanup
Use try/finally to ensure cleanup in finally block
explain: try/finally ensures cleanup code executes regardless of success or failure, preventing file accumulation and /tmp exhaustion across invocations.

q: You need to prevent duplicate processing of SQS messages. Currently some messages are processed twice. What should you implement?
options: Use FIFO queues only
Increase Lambda concurrency
Implement idempotency using message IDs and a tracking table
Decrease SQS visibility timeout
explain: Idempotency using message ID tracking in DynamoDB ensures that duplicate message delivery does not result in duplicate processing.

q: Your Lambda function needs to make 100+ API calls to gather data for a response. Each call takes 100ms. Current approach is too slow. What should you refactor to?
options: Increase Lambda memory and timeout
Cache results in ElastiCache
Make calls in parallel using Promise.all()
Move to Step Functions
explain: Parallel API calls using Promise.all() can reduce 10+ seconds of sequential time to ~100ms, vastly improving performance.

q: SQS batch processing shows some messages failing. Should you delete failed messages?
options: Delete immediately
Report partial batch failure to continue with next batch
Send to DLQ and retry
Depends on error type
explain: Failed messages should either be retried, sent to DLQ, or requeued based on error type.

q: You need to create an API that accepts large file uploads. What is the maximum request payload size for API Gateway?
options: 50 MB
100 MB
10 MB
1 MB
explain: API Gateway supports request payloads up to 10 MB; larger files should use S3 pre-signed URLs.

q: Your API Gateway REST API has CORS enabled but requests from a web app still fail with CORS errors. What headers should be checked?
options: Content-Type header
X-API-Key header
Access-Control-Allow-Origin and Access-Control-Allow-Methods response headers
Authorization header only
explain: CORS requires proper response headers including Access-Control-Allow-Origin, Methods, and Headers for browser requests.

q: You are deploying an application using Terraform. Should you use Terraform or CloudFormation?
options: Neither is suitable
Both are equivalent, choose based on preference
CloudFormation only
Terraform only
explain: Both are IaC tools; choose based on your infrastructure needs and team expertise.

q: A Lambda function uses environment variables to store a third-party API key and a database password. A security audit flags these as insufficiently protected — the values are visible in plaintext in the Lambda console and can be read by anyone with Lambda:GetFunctionConfiguration access. Which approach provides the strongest protection for these secrets at rest and limits exposure?
options: Use AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store Standard parameters to store the secrets, which are free and automatically encrypted at rest with no additional configuration
Encrypt the environment variables at rest using the Lambda default service key, which automatically encrypts all environment variables without additional configuration
Store the API key and database password in AWS Secrets Manager; retrieve them at function startup using the AWS SDK; cache the values in-memory for the function lifetime to minimize API calls
Base64-encode the secrets before storing them as environment variables to obfuscate the values from casual inspection in the console
explain: AWS Secrets Manager provides the strongest protection: secrets are encrypted with KMS, access is controlled by IAM policies independently of Lambda IAM, automatic rotation is supported, and the values are never stored in the Lambda function configuration where they could be exposed via Lambda:GetFunctionConfiguration. Caching the value in-memory during the function lifetime reduces Secrets Manager API calls. Lambda's default service encryption still exposes plaintext values to anyone with Lambda:GetFunctionConfiguration. Base64 is not encryption and provides no security. SSM Parameter Store Standard is valid but lacks automatic rotation; Secrets Manager is the AWS-recommended choice for credentials.

q: An API Gateway endpoint processes requests from multiple clients with different API keys for billing/tracking purposes. How should keys be managed?
options: Include keys in URL query parameter
Use Usage Plans associated with API Keys; API Gateway validates keys per request
Embed keys in Lambda function
Use only Basic Auth with username/password
explain: Usage Plans + API Keys enable per-client quota tracking, rate limiting, and billing without modifying Lambda logic.

q: You deploy a new API version but existing clients still call the old version. How should multiple versions coexist with different backends?
options: Use API Gateway stages (dev, prod, v2) with different integrations or stage variables for version routing
Redirect all requests to new version
Deploy new API with different domain
Deprecate old version immediately
explain: API Gateway stages + stage variables enable version management and gradual migration without breaking existing clients.

q: Amazon API Gateway logs reveal that a single client IP address is sending 10,000 requests per minute to a public REST API, causing throttling for legitimate users. The traffic pattern matches a credential-stuffing attack. The team needs to block this IP immediately while maintaining access for all other users. Which combination of actions achieves this most directly?
options: Associate AWS WAF with the API Gateway stage and create a WAF IP set rule that blocks the specific client IP address
Deploy a Lambda authorizer that queries a DynamoDB blocklist table on every request and returns a 403 Deny policy for the offending IP
Increase the API Gateway account-level throttling limit so legitimate requests are not affected by the high-volume IP
Enable API Gateway usage plans and assign the offending IP a usage plan with a rate limit of 0 requests per minute
explain: AWS WAF integrates directly with API Gateway and allows creating IP set-based rules that block specific IP addresses at the edge before requests reach the API. This is the fastest, most targeted solution — the block takes effect within seconds of updating the WAF IP set. API Gateway usage plans operate per API key, not per IP address. Increasing the throttling limit makes the problem worse for legitimate users. A Lambda authorizer adds latency to every request and is operationally complex for simple IP blocking.

q: You need to parse CloudFormation custom resource requests in Lambda. What is the response format?
options: CloudFormation event JSON
JSON with Status, PhysicalResourceId, Data fields sent to S3
Standard HTTP 200
Any JSON response
explain: Custom resource Lambda handlers must respond with specific JSON format including Status, PhysicalResourceId.

q: You want to automatically assign new Cognito users to a default group upon signup. What trigger should be configured?
options: Pre-sign-up trigger
Manual group assignment only
Post-confirmation trigger to add user to group after email verification
Custom authentication flow
explain: Post-confirmation trigger fires after user confirms email; use it to programmatically add user to default group.

q: Your Lambda function needs to access database passwords. Currently they are stored in environment variables. What is the secure alternative?
options: Store in Lambda code
Store encrypted in environment variables
Use Secrets Manager with automatic rotation
Store in S3
explain: Secrets Manager provides automatic credential rotation, audit logging, and fine-grained IAM permissions without exposing secrets in environment variables.

q: A developer needs to obtain temporary AWS credentials for an authenticated Cognito user to access S3. What Cognito feature is used?
options: IAM users for each Cognito user
Use Cognito Identity Pool (federated identity) to exchange Cognito token for AWS temporary credentials
User Pool alone provides AWS credentials
Direct S3 access without credentials
explain: Cognito Identity Pool exchanges ID token for temporary AWS credentials with scoped permissions via IAM role.

q: You are implementing Cognito authentication for your API. Users should be able to authenticate via their corporate directory. Which Cognito feature enables this?
options: Lambda authorizer
Cognito user pools alone
Cognito identity pools
Identity providers (IdP) integration with Cognito user pools
explain: Cognito user pools support identity provider federation, allowing users to authenticate with SAML/OAuth providers like corporate directories.

q: Your API requires mutual TLS (mTLS) for client authentication. How should you implement this in API Gateway?
options: Enable mutual authentication with client certificates
Use Lambda authorizers
Use Cognito instead
Configure AWS WAF IP allowlist rules to restrict API access to known client IPs
explain: API Gateway client certificate authentication validates client certificates and can pass certificate info to Lambda authorizers for authorization decisions.

q: Your Lambda function calls KMS to decrypt sensitive data on every invocation. This adds latency. How should you optimize?
options: Use Data Key Caching with the KMS SDK
Use Secrets Manager instead
Cache decrypted values in memory within the Lambda container
Disable KMS encryption
explain: KMS data key caching reduces API calls to KMS by reusing cached plaintext keys for a configurable period, improving performance and reducing costs.

q: Your application authenticates users with Cognito User Pool and needs to know the user's email and custom attributes. Where should this information be retrieved?
options: User attributes are not accessible after login
From the ID token claims
Query DynamoDB for user attributes
Call GetUser API to fetch user profile with email and custom attributes
explain: ID token JWT contains user attributes as claims (email, custom attributes); decode it directly without extra API calls.

q: You need to ensure that developers cannot accidentally commit AWS credentials to your Git repository. What should you implement?
options: Manual code review
Client-side git hooks to scan for secrets
Use services like git-secrets or truffleHog
Store credentials in .gitignore
explain: Automated secret scanning tools like truffleHog or git-secrets can detect credential patterns and prevent commits containing secrets.

q: Your ECS task needs to pull a private Docker image from ECR. What must be configured?
options: ECR repository permissions
B and optionally C
ECS task execution role with ECR permissions
VPC endpoint for ECR
explain: Task execution role needs ECR read permissions; VPC endpoint is optional if using public ECR.

q: A public REST API deployed on API Gateway is receiving requests from IPs performing automated vulnerability scanning. The security team requires blocking these IPs in real time while allowing legitimate traffic. Which solution provides IP-based blocking with the LEAST management overhead?
options: Deploy AWS WAF with an IP set rule attached to the API Gateway stage — add scanning IPs to the IP set; WAF evaluates every request before it reaches the API
Create a usage plan with a very low throttle limit and associate all API keys with that plan — attackers who do not send an API key will be blocked automatically
Enable VPC Link for the API and restrict inbound traffic using security group rules on the VPC, dropping traffic from scanning IPs at the network layer
Configure API Gateway resource policies with explicit Deny statements for known malicious IP ranges and redeploy the API after each update
explain: AWS WAF attached to an API Gateway stage evaluates every incoming request against IP set rules before the request reaches the backend. Adding an IP to the WAF IP set blocks it immediately without any API redeployment. WAF supports both IPv4 and IPv6, allows rule updates in near-real time, and provides detailed request logs for forensics. API Gateway resource policies also support IP restrictions but require an API redeployment after each change, making them less suitable for dynamic blocking. Usage plans require API keys and do not block unauthenticated scanners. VPC Link changes the network architecture significantly.

q: You need to pass large context data between Lambda functions. What should you use?
options: Pass in event data directly
Use S3 with event containing key
Store in DynamoDB and pass key reference
B or C depending on size
explain: For large data, store in DynamoDB/S3 and pass references to avoid Lambda payload limits.

q: You implement Cognito authentication with app client settings requiring a callback URL. What is this for?
options: OAuth callback URL where Cognito sends authorization code after user approves; app exchanges code for tokens
Health check endpoint
Redirect after logout only
Not needed for User Pool
explain: Callback URL is the OAuth redirect endpoint; Cognito sends auth code there, app exchanges it for tokens.

q: A development team deploys a microservice that applies Flyway database migrations as part of every release. The current pipeline runs migrations and application deployment in a single CodeDeploy step, causing failures when a migration succeeds but the application deploy fails — leaving the database in a newer schema state than the running application. Which pipeline design prevents this inconsistency?
options: Remove explicit migration steps and rely on Flyway's built-in application startup migration, triggering it on the first healthy request after deployment
Run migrations after the application deployment completes, so the application always starts with the old schema and migrates immediately after startup
Run migrations and deployment in a single atomic CodeDeploy lifecycle hook so both operations succeed or fail together
Add a dedicated pipeline stage before the deploy stage that runs migrations using a Lambda function or ECS task; only advance to the deploy stage if migrations exit successfully
explain: A dedicated pre-deploy migration stage ensures the database schema is upgraded before any application code runs. If the migration fails, the pipeline stops and the old application version continues running against the old schema — a consistent state. If migrations succeed and the deploy fails, the schema is ahead of the application, but forward-compatible migrations (additive-only changes) prevent breakage, making this the safest pattern. Running migrations after deployment means the new application starts against the old schema, causing startup failures. A single atomic step is not possible because CodeDeploy and Flyway are separate systems. Startup-time migrations delay first request and make failures harder to diagnose.

q: A Cognito User Pool is integrated with a third-party identity provider (OIDC). Users logging in via OIDC should have specific IAM permissions. How are these assigned?
options: Permissions cannot be granted for external identity providers
Use Cognito Identity Pool with role mapping to assign IAM role based on external provider
Create separate IAM users for each provider
OIDC automatically grants permissions
explain: Identity Pool role mapping associates IAM roles with OIDC provider attributes, granting AWS permissions after external auth.

q: Your Cognito user pool has a password policy requiring special characters. A user's password reset email contains a temporary password. Is this temporary password subject to the policy?
options: No policy check is performed
No, temporary passwords bypass policy
Temporary passwords are invalid after 1 hour
Yes, temporary passwords must still meet policy requirements and user must change it on first login
explain: Temporary passwords meet policy requirements and must be changed on first login for security.

q: A Lambda function reads JSON Web Tokens (JWT) from API requests and currently validates the JWT signature manually inside the function code. A security team review recommends centralizing this validation. Which approach is MORE SECURE and reduces duplicated validation logic?
options: Validate the JWT signature manually inside each Lambda function — this gives the most control and avoids API Gateway dependency

Use an API Gateway Lambda authorizer (TOKEN type) to validate the JWT signature and claims before the request reaches the Lambda function — unauthorized requests are rejected at the API Gateway layer without invoking the backend Lambda
Store all valid JWT tokens in a DynamoDB table and compare each incoming token against the stored values to validate authenticity
Skip JWT validation entirely and rely on network-level security (VPC, security groups) to ensure only trusted clients call the API
explain: A Lambda authorizer at the API Gateway level validates JWTs once before routing to any backend function, centralizing security logic and preventing unauthorized invocations. This reduces duplicated validation code, improves performance (rejected requests never invoke backend Lambdas), and provides a single enforcement point. Skipping validation entirely is a critical security vulnerability. Manual per-function validation works but creates code duplication and means unauthorized requests still invoke and are billed for the Lambda execution. Storing tokens in DynamoDB creates a custom token registry that doesn't validate cryptographic signatures.

q: You need to log API request/response bodies for debugging but must not log sensitive fields like passwords. How should you implement this?
options: Log everything
Store logs in S3 encrypted
Use VPC Flow Logs instead
Do not log sensitive data; filter/mask it in CloudWatch Logs
explain: Implement field masking/filtering in logging code to prevent sensitive data from being written to CloudWatch Logs and accessible to other teams.

q: An API Gateway uses a Lambda authorizer with a 5-minute cache TTL. A user's account is compromised and the security team immediately revokes the user's JWT token in the identity provider. The token was valid and cached by the authorizer 2 minutes ago. What is the security risk?
options: The Lambda authorizer cache is scoped per IAM role, not per token — revoking a user does not affect the cache entry because the cache key is the role ARN
API Gateway automatically clears the authorizer cache when a token is revoked via the Cognito admin API, eliminating the security gap
The Lambda authorizer will re-invoke for the next request regardless of cache — revoked tokens are always re-validated because the cache only stores the result for the first call
The revoked token remains valid for up to 3 more minutes (remainder of the 5-minute TTL) — an attacker holding the stolen token can continue making authorized API calls during this window
explain: Lambda authorizer caching stores the IAM policy returned for a given token for the duration of the TTL. If a token is revoked at the identity provider after the authorizer has already cached a valid policy for it, subsequent requests using that token bypass re-validation and are authorized for the remaining cache duration. In this scenario, that is up to 3 more minutes. The remediation options are: reduce the TTL to shorten the exposure window, disable caching for high-security operations, or implement a token revocation check (e.g., against a DynamoDB denylist) inside the Lambda authorizer itself.

q: What is the difference between authentication headers and authorization headers?
options: Both are optional
Only one is necessary
Authentication proves who you are, authorization proves what you can do
They are the same
explain: Authentication verifies identity; authorization determines resource access permissions.

q: You need to call AWS APIs from a JavaScript web application. Using IAM user credentials in JavaScript is insecure. What should you use?
options: Store credentials in localStorage
Cognito Identity pools to obtain temporary STS credentials
Root account credentials
Hardcode IAM user API access keys in the JavaScript bundle and rotate them quarterly
explain: Cognito Identity pools provide temporary AWS credentials with limited permissions, avoiding the need for long-term credentials in client applications.

q: Your application needs to integrate with a third-party service that requires API key authentication. How should you store this key securely in your Lambda?
options: Hardcode in Lambda function
Pass via API Gateway
Store in environment variable
Store in AWS Secrets Manager
explain: Secrets Manager provides encryption, audit logging, automatic rotation support, and fine-grained access control for third-party credentials.

q: A team manages a single CodePipeline that must deploy the same application artifact to three environments: dev, staging, and production. Each environment lives in a separate AWS account. The pipeline must require manual approval before promoting to production. Which CodePipeline design correctly supports multi-account deployments?
options: Use a single CodePipeline in the tooling account with CodeDeploy deployment actions for each environment; configure cross-account IAM roles that the pipeline assumes to deploy into each target account; add a Manual Approval action before the production stage
Deploy to each environment by uploading artifacts to account-specific S3 buckets and triggering account-local CodeDeploy agents via S3 event notifications
Create three separate CodePipelines, one per account, and trigger each sequentially using EventBridge events when the previous pipeline succeeds
Use AWS Organizations Service Control Policies to grant the source account's CodePipeline service role access to all member accounts without needing per-account IAM roles
explain: AWS CodePipeline supports cross-account deployments natively. The pipeline lives in a central tooling account; each deploy action assumes a cross-account IAM role in the target account that has the permissions needed for CodeDeploy, ECS, Lambda, or CloudFormation. A Manual Approval action gates the production deploy. This single-pipeline design gives end-to-end visibility, enforces promotion order, and maintains a single source of truth for the pipeline definition. Three separate pipelines lose cross-environment visibility and make coordinated rollbacks harder. S3-triggered deployments bypass CodePipeline's approval gates. SCPs restrict permissions but do not grant them — cross-account roles are still required.

q: A REST API built with API Gateway and Lambda allows authenticated users to retrieve their own profile data via GET /users/{userId}. Currently any authenticated user can pass any userId and retrieve another user's profile. The development team must enforce resource ownership — users may only access their own profile. Which Lambda authorizer implementation enforces this correctly?
options: Use API Gateway resource policies to restrict access by IP address range, preventing unauthorized users from reaching the endpoint
In the Lambda function handler, compare the userId path parameter against the sub claim extracted from the verified Cognito JWT in the Authorization header; return a 403 response if they do not match
Validate that the JWT token is not expired in the Lambda authorizer and return an Allow policy — rely on the database query to filter results by the authenticated user's ID
Enable API Gateway IAM authorization and require each client to sign requests with their own IAM credentials, mapping IAM principal to userId in a DynamoDB lookup table
explain: Resource ownership checks must happen inside the Lambda function handler after the JWT is verified. The handler extracts the authenticated user's sub (subject) claim from the verified JWT and compares it to the userId path parameter. If they differ, the function returns HTTP 403 without executing the data query. A Lambda authorizer that only validates token expiry does not prevent horizontal privilege escalation — it grants access to any valid token regardless of which user ID is requested. IP-based resource policies do not identify individual users. IAM authorization works for machine-to-machine calls but is impractical for end-user web/mobile apps.

q: Your API Gateway uses OAuth 2.0 for authentication. How should you validate the access token in your Lambda?
options: Trust the token from API Gateway without validation
Call the OAuth provider to validate
Validate token signature and expiration in Lambda
Use CloudFront to validate
explain: Validate token signature using the OAuth provider public keys and check token expiration in Lambda to prevent use of expired or tampered tokens.

q: A message remains in SQS queue for 2 hours after processing but before being deleted. What is the issue?
options: The queue is broken
Message retention period is set to 2 hours
Consumer received message but didn't delete it; visibility timeout expired and message became visible again
SQS requires manual deletion
explain: Visibility timeout hides message from other consumers; consumer must delete it within timeout or it becomes visible again.

q: A platform team manages infrastructure for a SaaS product using AWS CodePipeline. The pipeline must deploy CloudFormation stacks to a development account and, after manual approval, to a production account — both accounts are separate from the tooling account where CodePipeline runs. The team wants one pipeline definition with no manual artifact promotion steps. Which setup is correct?
options: Use CodeDeploy deployment groups with separate on-premises instance tags for dev and prod servers, avoiding the need for cross-account permissions
Configure AWS Organizations delegated administrator for CodePipeline so the single pipeline can deploy to all member accounts without per-account IAM role setup
Create two separate CodePipelines — one in the dev account and one in the prod account — triggered sequentially via EventBridge cross-account events after each successful deployment

Use a single CodePipeline in the tooling account with CloudFormation deploy actions; configure each action to assume a cross-account CloudFormation deployment role in the respective target account; add a Manual Approval action between the dev and prod deploy stages
explain: A single CodePipeline in the tooling account can deploy to multiple AWS accounts using cross-account IAM roles. Each CloudFormation deploy action specifies a RoleArn — the role in the target account that CloudFormation assumes to create/update resources. The pipeline also needs an artifact encryption key shared between accounts (cross-account KMS key or S3 bucket policy). A Manual Approval action between stages enforces the dev-before-prod gate. Two separate pipelines lose unified visibility and require external coordination. AWS Organizations does not provide a CodePipeline delegated admin feature. CodeDeploy on-premises does not apply to CloudFormation-managed AWS infrastructure.

q: Your SQS queue has a visibility timeout of 5 seconds but processing takes 10 seconds. What happens?
options: Processing completes successfully; timeout doesn't interrupt
Processing is killed after 5 seconds
Message is deleted automatically
After 5 seconds, message becomes visible to other consumers before first consumer finishes; potential duplicate processing
explain: Low visibility timeout causes duplicate processing; increase timeout to exceed max processing time.

q: Your CodeBuild project builds Docker images but the build times are increasing. After investigation, you find Docker layer caching is not working. What should you configure?
options: Use buildspec.yml with docker buildx
Enable Docker layer caching in CodeBuild build environment
Upgrade CodeBuild to newer version
Use S3 for build caching
explain: CodeBuild supports Docker layer caching by storing intermediate layers. Enable this in the project settings to reuse layers across builds.

q: A team uses CodeDeploy to deploy to a fleet of 20 EC2 instances. During the latest deployment, 4 instances fail the ApplicationStop lifecycle hook. The team wants to maximize deployment speed while ensuring at least 15 instances remain healthy throughout the deployment. Which deployment configuration achieves this?
options: Create a custom deployment configuration with MinimumHealthyHosts set to 15 (fixed count) — CodeDeploy deploys to at most 5 instances at a time and stops if healthy hosts drop below 15
Use the CodeDeployDefault.OneAtATime configuration — deploy to one instance at a time to isolate failures, accepting slower deployment speed
Use the CodeDeployDefault.HalfAtATime configuration — deploys to 10 instances at a time, keeping 10 healthy, which satisfies the 15-host minimum
Use the CodeDeployDefault.AllAtOnce configuration with automatic rollback disabled — deploy to all instances simultaneously and manually fix failures afterward
explain: A custom deployment configuration with MinimumHealthyHosts = 15 (count) instructs CodeDeploy to keep at least 15 instances in a healthy state at all times. With 20 total instances, CodeDeploy will deploy to at most 5 instances concurrently. If failures cause healthy instances to drop below 15, the deployment stops and can trigger automatic rollback. This directly satisfies the requirement. CodeDeployDefault.HalfAtATime deploys to 10 at once, leaving only 10 healthy — below the 15-instance requirement. AllAtOnce risks dropping all instances. OneAtATime is too slow with no minimum-healthy guarantee.

q: An SQS consumer needs to process messages in the exact order they were sent. What should be configured?
options: Standard queue processes in FIFO order
Use FIFO queue with message group ID for ordering guarantee
Enable message ordering in attributes
Order is not guaranteed in SQS
explain: Only SQS FIFO queues guarantee message order; standard queues do not maintain order.

q: You configure a dead letter queue (DLQ) for an SQS queue with max receive count=3. A message fails 3 times. What happens?
options: After 3 failed receive attempts, message is moved to DLQ for analysis
Message is retried indefinitely
Message is deleted
Processing stops for the queue
explain: Messages reaching max receive count are moved to DLQ; process DLQ messages for failure analysis and recovery.

q: Your Elastic Beanstalk environment uses the default deployment policy but deployments cause downtime. Which deployment policy would eliminate downtime?
options: Immutable or Blue/Green
Rolling with additional batch
Rolling
All at once (current)
explain: Rolling, immutable, and blue/green deployments maintain availability during updates. All at once causes brief downtime.

q: Your application needs to guarantee that each SQS message is processed exactly once. What pattern ensures this?
options: Disable retries
Exactly-once guarantee is impossible
Standard queue is sufficient
Use FIFO queue + idempotent processing logic + message deduplication ID
explain: FIFO + idempotency (tracking processed message IDs) guarantees exactly-once semantics despite potential retries.

q: Your CodeDeploy configuration uses Canary traffic shifting. 5% of traffic goes to new version; if no errors in 10 minutes, remaining 95% shifts. What is the benefit?
options: Cost reduction
Faster deployments
Early error detection with minimal blast radius; rollback only affects 5%
No rollback needed
explain: Canary deployment minimizes blast radius; if new version fails, only 5% of traffic is affected before automatic rollback.

q: A CodeDeploy EC2 deployment fails because the CodeDeploy agent is not running on the instance. How should this be configured?
options: CodeDeploy doesn't need an agent on EC2
CodeDeploy agent should be pre-installed and running on all EC2 instances
Agent should be installed manually after each deployment
CodeDeploy automatically installs the agent on first deployment
explain: CodeDeploy agent must be pre-installed and running on EC2 instances; it pulls and executes deployment scripts.

q: A team builds several Lambda functions that share a common set of Python utility libraries totaling 45 MB. Each function's deployment package is 60 MB — well above the 50 MB direct upload limit — because the shared libraries are bundled with every function. The team uses AWS SAM. How should they restructure the SAM template to stay under the size limit and avoid duplicating the shared libraries?
options: Use Docker container images instead of zip packages for each Lambda function and push them to Amazon ECR, which supports images up to 10 GB

Define a separate AWS::Serverless::LayerVersion resource in the SAM template containing the shared Python libraries; upload the layer to S3 and reference it in each function's Layers property — the layer is not counted toward the function's 50 MB direct upload limit
Enable Lambda SnapStart on each function to cache the initialization phase, which reduces the effective deployment package size below the limit
Split each Lambda function into two smaller functions and chain them with Step Functions to distribute the library load across multiple packages
explain: Lambda Layers are designed exactly for this scenario. In SAM, you define an AWS::Serverless::LayerVersion resource that packages the shared libraries, upload it to S3 (or let SAM manage the upload), and reference the layer ARN in each function's Layers property. The layer content is not included in the function's deployment package size, so each function package drops from 60 MB to ~15 MB — well under the 50 MB limit. Multiple functions share one copy of the layer. SnapStart reduces cold start time but does not change package size. Splitting functions adds coordination complexity. Container images work but require switching from zip to container workflow, which is a larger architectural change than adding a layer.

q: Your CodeDeploy deployment includes a custom deployment script in AppSpec that modifies application files. The script fails but deployment continues. What should be configured?
options: Script failures are ignored
Deployment always continues despite script failures
Manually intervene to stop deployment
Set OnFailure=STOP in the hook configuration to stop deployment on script failure
explain: AppSpec hooks can be configured with OnFailure=STOP to halt deployment if validation or setup scripts fail.

q: A platform team uses AWS CDK to manage infrastructure across two stacks: a DatabaseStack that provisions an Aurora cluster and an ApplicationStack that runs an ECS service needing the Aurora cluster endpoint. The ApplicationStack is deployed after DatabaseStack and must reference the cluster endpoint without hardcoding it. Which CDK pattern correctly passes outputs between stacks in the same CDK app?
options: Use a Lambda-backed custom resource in ApplicationStack that calls the RDS DescribeClusters API at deploy time to look up the cluster endpoint dynamically

Store the cluster endpoint in AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store from the DatabaseStack using a custom CloudFormation resource; read it from SSM in the ApplicationStack using ParameterStoreValue.valueFromLookup()

Pass the Aurora cluster construct (or its endpoint property) as a constructor parameter from the DatabaseStack instance to the ApplicationStack instance in the CDK app entry point, enabling type-safe cross-stack references resolved at synth time
Export the endpoint from DatabaseStack using CfnOutput with exportName; import it in ApplicationStack using Fn.importValue(), creating a hard CloudFormation stack dependency
explain: The CDK-native pattern for cross-stack references within the same app is to pass constructs or their properties as constructor parameters. The CDK app instantiates DatabaseStack first, then passes dbStack.cluster.clusterEndpoint.hostname to the ApplicationStack constructor. CDK automatically creates a CloudFormation Export in DatabaseStack and an Import in ApplicationStack, wiring the dependency correctly. This is type-safe and refactor-friendly. SSM ParameterStoreValue.valueFromLookup() runs at synth time and may not reflect the latest deployed value. Fn.importValue() with CfnOutput works but is the lower-level approach that CDK's cross-stack construct reference model already handles automatically. A custom resource Lambda adds operational complexity unnecessarily.

q: A CodeDeploy deployment to EC2 instances fails with 'permission denied' when the AppSpec lifecycle hook script attempts to write an artifact to an S3 bucket. The EC2 instances use an IAM instance profile. The S3 bucket has a bucket policy. The CodeDeploy service role has S3 read access. Which investigation step identifies the root cause?
options: Confirm the CodeDeploy agent version on the EC2 instances is up to date, as older agent versions have known S3 write permission bugs
Check whether the CodeDeploy deployment group is configured with the correct revision location (S3 bucket and key) for the application bundle
Check if the S3 bucket has versioning enabled, as CodeDeploy requires versioning to be disabled before writing deployment artifacts

Verify the IAM instance profile attached to the EC2 instances includes s3:PutObject permission for the destination bucket — CodeDeploy lifecycle scripts run as the instance's IAM role, not the CodeDeploy service role
explain: CodeDeploy lifecycle hook scripts execute on the EC2 instance under the instance's IAM role (the instance profile), not under the CodeDeploy service role. The service role is used by the CodeDeploy control plane to read deployment bundles and report status — it is not involved in what the hook scripts can do. Therefore, if a hook script writes to S3, the EC2 instance profile must have s3:PutObject permission for that bucket. A missing s3:PutObject permission on the instance profile is the most common cause of this exact error. The revision location configuration affects bundle download, not hook script write operations. Agent version bugs are rare and should be checked after IAM is verified. S3 versioning state does not affect PutObject permissions.

q: Your Elastic Beanstalk environment needs to execute initialization scripts. Where should these be placed?
options: In boot scripts manually
Not possible in Beanstalk
In .ebextensions/commands.config
In application code
explain: .ebextensions directory allows you to define commands, packages, and configuration that execute during environment creation and updates.

q: You need to implement feature flags for gradual rollout. What service should you use?
options: AppConfig for centralized feature flag management
Lambda environment variables
DynamoDB configuration table
S3 configuration files
explain: AppConfig provides managed feature flag and configuration service with client library.

q: You need to deploy an updated Lambda function version but maintain backward compatibility. Clients still use the old version. What should you do?
options: Create new version and use aliases to control which version gets traffic
Use CloudFront versioning
Update function and increment version number manually
Delete old version
explain: Lambda aliases can point to different versions. Use gradual traffic shifting with aliases to migrate clients to new versions safely.

q: Your CodePipeline has CodeBuild testing stage that generates a test report. You need to visualize test results. What should CodeBuild configure?
options: Upload report to S3
Configure a Lambda function to email test results to developers after each build
Report groups with test report output
CloudWatch Logs only
explain: CodeBuild report groups generate test reports visible in CodePipeline and CodeBuild console, showing pass/fail/skipped test counts.

q: Your CloudFormation template has circular dependency: Resource A depends on B, B depends on A. How is this resolved?
options: Refactor template to break dependency cycle; use DependsOn only when implicit dependencies don't work
CloudFormation automatically detects and resolves circular dependencies
Create separate stacks for A and B
Circular dependencies are impossible
explain: CloudFormation cannot resolve circular dependencies; refactor template structure to eliminate them.

q: You are deploying containerized applications with CloudFormation. You need to update container image without updating the CloudFormation stack. What should you use?
options: Redeploy entire stack
Restart containers manually
Update ECS service to pull new image tag
Update the stack
explain: ECS services can be updated to pull new image versions independently of CloudFormation stack updates using the UpdateService API.

q: A CloudFormation stack creation fails partway through. You fix the issue and try to create again with the same stack name. What happens?
options: New stack is created with a different name
Creation succeeds with the corrected template
CloudFormation automatically cleans up and retries
Stack is in CREATE_FAILED state and must be deleted before recreating
explain: Failed stacks must be deleted before creating a new stack with the same name to avoid resource conflicts.

q: A development team wants to test their SAM application locally before deploying to AWS. The app has a Lambda function triggered by API Gateway that writes to a DynamoDB table. Developers need to run the full flow — HTTP request → Lambda → DynamoDB — on their laptops without incurring AWS charges for every test run. Which local testing approach works with AWS SAM?
options: Use sam local start-api to run a local API Gateway emulator that invokes Lambda functions locally; run a local DynamoDB instance using the DynamoDB Local Docker image; configure the Lambda function to point to localhost DynamoDB via an environment variable
Use AWS Cloud9 as the development environment so that Lambda and DynamoDB requests never leave the AWS network and latency is eliminated
Deploy a development stack to AWS using sam deploy --guided and use AWS-provided free tier resources for testing, which incur no charges under normal usage levels
Mock all AWS service calls in unit tests using the moto Python library and run sam local invoke for integration tests, bypassing the need for any local service emulation
explain: AWS SAM CLI provides sam local start-api, which starts a local HTTP server that emulates API Gateway and invokes Lambda functions in a Docker container on the developer's laptop. DynamoDB Local (available as a Docker image) emulates the DynamoDB API locally. By setting an environment variable (AWS_ENDPOINT_URL or a custom endpoint) the Lambda code points to localhost DynamoDB instead of the AWS endpoint, enabling a full local test loop without AWS charges. Deploying to AWS for testing incurs costs and adds deployment latency. Cloud9 runs in AWS and still makes real AWS API calls. moto is excellent for unit tests but does not emulate the API Gateway → Lambda invocation chain.

q: Your SAM template defines multiple Lambda functions sharing common code. How should shared code be packaged?
options: Duplicate code in each function
Shared code is not possible
Package shared code as a Lambda layer; reference in Layers property
Use nested stacks for each function
explain: Lambda layers contain shared code/dependencies; multiple functions reference the same layer, reducing duplication.

q: A CodeDeploy blue/green deployment to an ECS service fails during the replacement task set's health check phase. The new version of the container cannot start because of a missing environment variable. The original task set (blue) is still running and serving traffic. The team needs to immediately abort the deployment and restore full traffic to the stable blue version. What is the fastest way to achieve this?
options: In the AWS CodeDeploy console, choose the active deployment and select 'Stop and roll back deployment'; CodeDeploy will shift all traffic back to the original task set and terminate the replacement task set
Push a hotfix commit to the source repository to trigger a new CodePipeline execution that deploys a corrected container image
Delete the ECS service and recreate it from the previous task definition revision using the AWS CLI
Manually update the Application Load Balancer target group to remove the replacement task set's targets and add the original task set's targets back
explain: For ECS blue/green deployments managed by CodeDeploy, the 'Stop and roll back deployment' action in the CodeDeploy console (or via the StopDeployment API with autoRollbackEnabled) is the fastest recovery path.
